topic: Part 5: Kill-Shot Binary Tests — post-EXP-082 review (NMP TLDR, body, closing summary patches)

queue_item.reason: EXP-082 integrated — NMP TLDR, body paragraph, and closing summary paragraph all rewritten.

prior_review: SEC-part5-rewrite.c2.json (2026-04-10T22:30:00Z) — reviewed EXP-057 integration. Identified 2 major, 6 moderate, 3 minor holes. EXP-082 specifically addressed: NMP math discrepancy (major), Dome Says column (major), opening paragraph inconsistency (moderate), missing closing paragraph (moderate), incorrect 30% falsification threshold in TLDR (moderate).
our_argument_summary: Part 5 presents six kill-shot tests under the dome's own binary rule. EXP-082 patched four specific problems: (1) Test 6 TLDR corrected from nonexistent '30% threshold' to actual '>50% for 3 consecutive years', added independent calculation showing 40.0% ≈ dome's 39.9%; (2) opening paragraph fixed to acknowledge NMP's self-reported status alongside pending tests; (3) Test 6 body paragraph rewritten with correct observed rate (~38 km/yr), matching error calculation, and verbatim falsification criterion; (4) new closing summary paragraph applying dome's binary rule to dome's data. The Dome Says column was also previously fixed (now shows 'PASS (39.9%)').

holes_found.description: Verdict paragraph still contains the debunked '30% falsification threshold.' Line reads: 'The NMP drift rate prediction (a) is currently failing at 39.9% error on the dome's own metrics, (b) exceeds the dome's own 30% falsification threshold...' — but the TLDR (same analysis, three paragraphs earlier) correctly states '>50% error for 3 consecutive years,' and the body paragraph says the tolerance was 'widened to 50%.' The dome's raw text confirms: 'Falsification: Error >50% for 3 consecutive years.' Three different threshold numbers now appear in the same analysis: 50% (TLDR and body), 30% (verdict), and the actual dome criterion (>50% for 3 consecutive years). This is devastating: (a) 39.9% does NOT exceed 50%, so saying 'exceeds the dome's own threshold' is factually wrong if we use the correct 50% figure; (b) a dome defender will quote our own text against itself — 'your TLDR says 50% but your verdict says 30%, which is it?' This is exactly the kind of self-contradictory numerics we criticize the dome for. EXP-082 fixed the TLDR and body but missed the verdict paragraph.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite verdict item (b) to: '(b) approaches the dome's own >50% falsification threshold (which requires >50% error for 3 consecutive years — a criterion so generous that nearly-half-wrong predictions still pass)' or delete item (b) entirely and rely on the body's discussion of the threshold. The current '30%' figure must be removed — it is fabricated, appearing nowhere in the dome's published material.

holes_found.description: Duplicate opening sentence in Test 6 body paragraph. The text reads: 'The dome's own tracking shows failure. The dome's own tracking shows substantial error.' — the old sentence ('shows failure') was not removed when EXP-082's replacement text ('shows substantial error') was spliced in. This is a build/merge artifact that survived to the published page. A reader will see the stutter immediately. More importantly, 'shows failure' contradicts our own body text which explains why the dome calls it PASS (39.9% within 50% tolerance). We should NOT characterize the dome as admitting failure when the dome explicitly says PASS.
holes_found.recommendation: Delete the first sentence ('The dome's own tracking shows failure.'). Keep only 'The dome's own tracking shows substantial error.' — this is the accurate framing per EXP-082.

holes_found.description: Opening paragraph says 'one (NMP drift rate) self-reports as failing by its own 39.9% error metric' — but the dome does NOT self-report as failing. The dome says PASS (39.9%). The summary table correctly shows 'PASS (39.9%)' in the Dome Says column (fixed by the c2 review). The opening prose now contradicts the table. EXP-082's proposed opening text was: 'three are listed as pending — though the dome's live monitoring dashboard separately reports the NMP drift rate test at 39.9% error, within its own (widened) 50% tolerance.' This was accurate. The current text substituted a different formulation that mischaracterizes the dome's position. A defender will point to our table (which says the dome says PASS) and our opening (which says the dome self-reports failure) and ask which one we actually believe.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite the opening to match EXP-082's proposed text, or something like: 'Two are claimed as confirmed; three are pending; one (NMP drift rate) self-reports as passing at 39.9% error — though only by widening the tolerance to 50% after predictions missed.' This accurately states the dome's position while still conveying the critique.

holes_found.description: TLDR deceleration math says '~18 km/yr/yr deceleration — roughly 18× the historical rate of ~1 km/yr/yr.' Computational verification: historical average deceleration from peak (~55 km/yr circa 2003) to present (~38 km/yr in 2025) over ~22 years = 0.77 km/yr/yr, not ~1 km/yr/yr. Using 0.77 gives a ratio of ~23×, not 18×. The TLDR is slightly generous to the dome by rounding the historical deceleration up and the ratio down. This isn't factually wrong (it's within order-of-magnitude territory), but the precision matters when we're criticizing the dome's math. More importantly, the target date is ambiguous: 'by 2027' could mean January 2027 (~1 year from 2026 observation) or December 2027 (~2 years). If December 2027, the deceleration needed is ~9 km/yr/yr (still implausible at ~12× historical), not 18. I could not find '<20 km/yr by 2027' in the dome's raw text files — it may be paraphrasing from the dome site or from the dashboard. If this target is not the dome's verbatim statement, we risk fabricating a more demanding test than the dome committed to.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify the '<20 km/yr by 2027' target against the dome's actual published text. If the dome says 'exponential deceleration continues,' the target may be from our interpretation of their formula, not their explicit claim. State explicitly whether 'by 2027' means January or December. Use the verified historical deceleration rate (~0.77 km/yr/yr from the 2003-2025 trend) rather than ~1 km/yr/yr to maintain precision.

holes_found.description: The new closing summary paragraph states the two confirmations are 'calibration artifacts (Sydney-Perth: calibrated to a known railway distance) or within measurement noise (Polaris: claimed excess smaller than the star's own polar offset).' For Sydney-Perth this is well-supported by the Test 1 analysis. For Polaris, the characterization as 'within measurement noise' is slightly imprecise — the claimed +0.27° is smaller than Polaris's polar offset (±0.66°) but also smaller than typical field measurement uncertainty (±0.2-0.5°). The summary captures the gist but a pedantic dome defender could object that polar offset and measurement noise are different error sources, and conflating them is sloppy. Additionally, the summary omits that the dome's OWN site marks the broader Polaris prediction as FALSIFIED (which is the most damaging point from Test 2).
holes_found.recommendation: Consider adding the self-falsification to the Polaris bullet: 'within measurement noise (Polaris: claimed excess smaller than the star's own polar offset, and the dome's broader Polaris-latitude prediction is self-marked FALSIFIED).' This strengthens the closing by invoking the dome's own admission.

holes_found.description: The body paragraph references 'IGRF-14 forward extrapolation from the documented deceleration since 2005 (peak ~55 km/yr → ~42 km/yr by 2020 → ~38 km/yr by 2025).' IGRF-14 was released in December 2024 with a secular variation model through 2030, but the specific intermediate values (~42 km/yr by 2020, ~38 km/yr by 2025) should be cross-checked against IGRF-14's actual SV coefficients rather than presented as if they are directly from IGRF-14. Livermore et al. (2020) documented ~50 km/yr circa 2017-2019 (not ~42 by 2020). The ~42 figure may come from a different source or a later measurement. Without a specific citation for the 2020 and 2025 intermediate values, these are approximations from the general trend rather than verified IGRF-14 outputs.
holes_found.recommendation: Either cite specific IGRF-14 data for the intermediate values, or soften to: 'consistent with the documented deceleration trend since the 2003-2005 peak: from ~55 km/yr to ~38 km/yr by 2025 (IGRF-14; Livermore et al., 2020, Nature Geoscience).'

holes_found.description: Residual issues from c2 review unaddressed by EXP-082 (noted for tracking, not EXP-082's scope): (1) Test 5 still a single-paragraph stub, (2) Polaris offset inconsistency between Test 2 (~0.66°) and WIN-065 (0.74°) vs actual 2026 value (~0.63°), (3) Aubert 2015 cited for localized SAA cell prediction when it forecasts global field decrease, (4) no Wait-and-See discussion for Eclipse 2026, (5) Williams 2016 seasonal figures unverified. These were identified in SEC-part5-rewrite.c2.json and remain open.

stronger_arguments.description: The closing paragraph should quantify the asymmetric scorecard more aggressively. The dome marks NMP as PASS at 39.9% error (50% tolerance) but elsewhere marks minor globe mismatches as FAIL at much tighter thresholds. Raw-text/08-live-power.txt shows: Schumann falsification at '>0.3 Hz for >1 hour' (never observed in recorded history), equatorial gravity at '>0.1% vs WGS84,' and SAA Decay at 20% tolerance. Side-by-side comparison of these thresholds shows the scoring is systematically biased: tighter thresholds where the dome is likely to pass, wider where it's likely to fail.

The 'consecutive years' reset loophole in the NMP falsification criterion deserves more emphasis. With an exponentially decelerating formula, the dome's prediction will occasionally dip closer to reality as the actual rate also decelerates, creating periodic years where error drops below 50%. Each such year resets the 3-year clock. This means even a systematically wrong model can escape falsification indefinitely as long as the error oscillates around the threshold. This is not a rigorous falsification criterion — it's a ratchet designed to prevent failure.
sources: EXP-082 anticipated_objections[3] — already drafted this argument but it's in the expansion, not yet in the published text

kernel_of_truth.description: EXP-082 is a well-crafted set of surgical fixes. The NMP math discrepancy — where our prior text used ~35 km/yr observed but cited the dome's 39.9% error (which implies ~38 km/yr) — was precisely identified and correctly resolved. The independent calculation now matches the dome's figure (40.0% ≈ 39.9%), which paradoxically strengthens our critique: we can agree on the arithmetic while disagreeing on whether 39.9% error constitutes a 'PASS.' The closing paragraph applying the dome's binary rule to the dome's data is rhetorically effective and factually grounded.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The fixes were necessary but the integration was incomplete: the verdict paragraph was missed, a duplicate sentence was left in, and the opening paragraph was modified to a formulation that contradicts the corrected table. A surgical patch that fixes three of four places where a number appears but misses the fourth creates a new inconsistency worse than the original — because now the text contradicts ITSELF rather than just contradicting the dome.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A dome defender reading this section would immediately notice: (1) 'Your TLDR says my threshold is 50%, your body paragraph says 50%, but your verdict says 30%. You can't even keep your own numbers straight while lecturing me about numerical precision.' (2) 'Your opening paragraph says I self-report as failing. I don't. My dashboard says PASS. Your own table says I say PASS. Why does your prose say the opposite of your table?' (3) 'You have two copies of the same opening sentence in your Test 6 analysis — one says failure, one says substantial error. Did anyone proofread this?' (4) 'Your deceleration claim says I need 18× historical deceleration to hit my target, but you admit you can't find my actual target in my published material. Are you critiquing a prediction I actually made, or one you invented?'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Fix the three factual errors immediately (30% in verdict, duplicate sentence, opening mischaracterization). These are one-line fixes that eliminate the strongest defender counterattacks. The deceleration math ambiguity is worth verifying but less urgent — even the most generous interpretation (9× historical) is still implausible. After fixes, defense_survives drops to 0-1.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: Polaris offset inconsistency (Test 2 ~0.66° vs WIN-065 0.74° vs actual ~0.63°) persists from c2 review but is not within EXP-082's scope. NMP observed rate now consistent at ~38 km/yr across TLDR, body, and independent calculation — EXP-082 successfully resolved the prior 35/38 discrepancy.

claims_checked: Implied observed rate from 39.9% error: 38.0 km/yr ✓
Dome falsification criterion: >50% for 3 consecutive years ✓ (raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 65)
Adaptive tolerance note: confirmed in dome's own text ✓ (line 293)
Deceleration needed for <20 km/yr by 2027: 18 km/yr in 1 year (if Jan 2027) or 9 km/yr/yr (if Dec 2027)
Historical deceleration: ~0.77 km/yr/yr (55 to 38 over 22 years) — TLDR says ~1 km/yr/yr (rounded up)
quantitative_verification.computations: Python verification confirms: 55×exp(-0.88) = 22.81 km/yr; 22.82/0.601 = 37.97 → ~38 km/yr; |22.8-38|/38 = 40.0%. The dome's 39.9% is reproduced. Historical deceleration (55-38)/22 = 0.77 km/yr/yr; the TLDR's '~1 km/yr/yr' is a 30% overstatement that makes the dome look slightly less bad (23× rather than 18×). All numbers in the TLDR and body paragraph are directionally correct; the 30% in the verdict paragraph is fabricated.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: (1) Verdict paragraph: '30% falsification threshold' is factually wrong — dome says 50%. (2) Historical deceleration: ~0.77 km/yr/yr, not ~1 km/yr/yr. (3) '<20 km/yr by 2027' target: could not verify in dome's raw text.

recommended_action_detail: Three urgent fixes: (1) Delete '30%' from verdict paragraph and replace with correct 50% criterion (CRITICAL). (2) Delete duplicate 'The dome's own tracking shows failure.' sentence from Test 6 body (MAJOR). (3) Rewrite opening paragraph to not claim the dome 'self-reports as failing' when the dome says PASS (MODERATE). These are one-line edits that eliminate the most exploitable vulnerabilities. Unrelated residuals from c2 (Test 5 stub, Polaris offset, Aubert citation, Wait-and-See) remain open and should be tracked separately.

Dome dashboard: 'PASS (39.9% error)' — confirmed in raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 257
Dome dashboard: 'Adaptive tolerance — originally tighter, widened when predictions missed' — confirmed in raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 258/293
Dome formula: rate = 55×exp(-0.08×(year-2015)) — confirmed in raw-text/08-live-power.txt line 64
Livermore et al. 2020 Nature Geoscience — valid citation for NMP acceleration/deceleration

'<20 km/yr by 2027' target — could not locate in dome's raw text files. May be paraphrased from dome's formula extrapolation or from a page not captured in raw-text/
